title: How India is Governed
blurb: The three tiers of government & separation of powers.

sections.head: Three tiers
sections.body: India is governed at three levels: the Union government, the state governments, and local self-government.

sections.head: Three organs
sections.body: Power is split across the Legislature, Executive and Judiciary so no single organ becomes too strong.

sections.head: Who does what
sections.body: The Union List, State List and Concurrent List divide subjects between Centre and States.

title: MP vs MLA vs Ward Member
blurb: Who represents you, and at which level.

sections.head: Your MP
sections.body: A Member of Parliament represents your Lok Sabha constituency in the national Parliament.

sections.head: Your MLA
sections.body: A Member of the Legislative Assembly represents your state constituency and works on state subjects.

sections.head: Your Ward Member / Sarpanch
sections.body: At the local level you elect ward members and a panchayat president or municipal councillor.

sections.head: You elect all three
sections.body: One citizen, three votes across different elections, each tier solving different problems.

title: 73rd & 74th Amendments
blurb: How local self-government got constitutional power.

sections.head: The big idea
sections.body: The 73rd Amendment gave constitutional status to Panchayats and the 74th to Municipalities.

sections.head: Guaranteed elections
sections.body: Both mandate elections every five years, conducted by a State Election Commission.

sections.head: Reservation
sections.body: Reserved seats for women and for SCs and STs make local politics more representative.

sections.head: The Gram Sabha
sections.body: The Gram Sabha is the assembly of all adult voters in a village and approves plans and budgets.

sections.head: 11th & 12th Schedules
sections.body: The 11th and 12th Schedules list subjects for panchayats and municipalities.

title: How a Bill Becomes Law
blurb: The journey from idea to Act of Parliament.

sections.head: Introduction
sections.body: A proposed law is a Bill. It can be introduced in either House, except Money Bills.

sections.head: Three readings
sections.body: Each House debates the Bill in stages before voting on it.

sections.head: President's assent
sections.body: After both Houses pass it, the Bill goes to the President and becomes an Act when assented.

sections.head: Why it matters
sections.body: This process ensures laws are debated, checked and revised before they become law.

title: Your Fundamental Rights
blurb: The six rights every Indian holds.

sections.head: Right to Equality
sections.body: Equality before law, non-discrimination and equal opportunity in public jobs.

sections.head: Right to Freedom
sections.body: Freedom of speech, assembly, movement, residence and profession.

sections.head: Against Exploitation
sections.body: Bans human trafficking, forced labour and hazardous child labour.

sections.head: Freedom of Religion
sections.body: Freedom to practise, profess and propagate any religion.

sections.head: Cultural & Educational
sections.body: Protects the language, script and culture of minorities.

sections.head: Right to Constitutional Remedies
sections.body: Article 32 lets you go to court if a fundamental right is violated.
